Study Page

Explaining the Inclusion Mind Map

This page hosts the inclusion image and explains the ideas around it: equity, belonging, community, respect, diversity, accessibility, student voice, and meaningful participation.

Belonging Equity UDL Child-honouring language Presume competency
中文提示

这张图的中心是 Inclusion（包容教育）。它强调学生不只是“在场”，而是有归属感、能参与、被尊重、能和同伴互动，并能得到公平的支持。

1. Main Idea

Inclusion means every student is welcomed, respected, supported, and able to participate meaningfully in the classroom and school community.

The mind map shows that inclusion is not just a place. A student can be physically present and still not feel included. True inclusion requires planning, relationships, access, respect, and meaningful learning opportunities.

Not true inclusionTrue inclusion
Only being in the same roomBeing part of the group and activity
“Breathing the same air”Participating with peers in a purposeful way
Doing separate work with an adultLearning the same topic at an appropriate level
Adults deciding everything for the studentStudent voice, choice, autonomy, and decision making

2. Key Branches in the Mind Map

Access

Equity, not just equality

Equality means everyone gets the same thing. Equity means students get what they need to access learning.

  • Remove barriers
  • Tailor resources and technology
  • Use UDL: Universal Design for Learning
  • Make sure needs are met
Participation

Being there is not enough

The map says inclusion is not just being there or breathing the same air. Students need purposeful placement and positive social interaction.

  • Purposeful placement
  • Positive social interactions
  • Friendship and peers
  • Meaningful participation
Dignity

Diversity and child-honouring language

Inclusion respects differences and focuses on strengths, not labels or deficits.

  • Person-centred thinking
  • Child-honouring language
  • Accepting differences
  • Strength-based support
Belonging

Community and belonging

Students should feel like they are part of the class community, not visitors or outsiders.

  • Belonging and friends
  • All students are responsible community members
  • Students contribute
  • Students have autonomy and choice
Mindset

Presume competency

The black branch reminds us that inclusion is a right and a principle, not a favour. Educators should presume competency and keep a growth mindset.

  • Right and entitlement
  • Not kindness or favour
  • Growth mindset
  • Positive regard
Relationships

Collaboration, choice, and safety

Inclusion depends on communication and teamwork with families, teachers, EAs, and the student.

  • Collaborate with family
  • Use communication methods and entry points
  • Create safety and confidence
  • Support fun and independence

3. How Inclusion May Look in the Classroom

Example 1: Same topic, different access

The class is researching penguins and making a poster. One student may label penguin body parts, choose pictures, identify two foods penguins eat, or use speech-to-text. The student is still connected to the same class topic and peer group.

Example 2: Student voice and choice

A student chooses whether to show learning by drawing, using pictures, recording audio, or working with a peer. The adult supports independence instead of doing the task for the student.

Example 3: Belonging and peers

The EA helps classmates interact naturally with the student, rather than becoming the student’s only partner. Peer friendships and positive social interactions are part of the plan.

Example 4: Removing barriers

Before a field trip, staff plan transportation, physical access, communication supports, and sensory needs so the student can attend with classmates.

4. Writing Help: A Short Paragraph

You can use this as a model for your inclusion writing assignment.

Inclusion means that every student is welcomed, respected, and supported to participate meaningfully in the classroom and school community. It is not just placing a student in the same room or letting them “breathe the same air.” True inclusion means removing barriers, using equitable supports, respecting differences, and helping students build friendships and independence. In the classroom, inclusion may look like students working on the same topic with different levels of support. For example, during a research project, one student may use pictures, assistive technology, or a modified task to show understanding while still learning with peers. An EA can support inclusion by encouraging communication, promoting independence, using respectful language, and helping the student feel valued as a member of the class.
中文理解

包容教育的重点不是“同一个房间”，而是学生能真正参与、有朋友、有选择、有尊严，并得到公平的支持。

5. Practice Questions

Question 1

Why does the image say inclusion is “not just being there”?

Because a student can be physically present but still separated, passive, or not connected with classmates. Inclusion requires meaningful participation, relationships, and access to learning.

Question 2

What is the difference between equality and equity?

Equality means everyone gets the same support. Equity means each student gets the support they need to access learning and participate.

Your own notes
